source_xlsx: MR Data\PET Resin Total landed cost calculator.xlsx

sample_non_empty_rows: This Excel calculator computes the Total Landed Cost (TLC) of vPET Resin imports across in-scope countries of this assesment. It accounts for FOB pricing, freight, insurance, import duties, and various local taxes (IGV, IPI, ICMS, etc.).

Note on 3907.60: This code was widely used in older versions of the HS nomenclature. However, in recent updates (HS 2017 and 2022), it was split into 3907.61 and 3907.69 to provide more detail on viscosity. You should generally avoid using 3907.60 unless you are filing in a country that has not updated its tariff schedule.
Import duty (Mercosur tariff but has been raised nationally in Brazil):
IPI (Imposto sobre Produtos Industrializados) is a federal excise tax on manufactured goods:
PIS (Programa de Integração Social (Social Integration Program) is a federal tax collected to fund unemployment insurance and employee allowances.
CONFINS (Contribuição para o Financiamento da Seguridade Social (Social Security Financing Contribution):

IPI (Imposto sobre Produtos Industrializados): This is a federal Excise Tax on Industrialized Products levied on manufactured goods at the point of production or customs clearance.

COFINS (Contribuição para o Financiamento da Seguridade Social): This is a federal Social Security Financing Contribution used to fund healthcare and public pensions, applied at a standard 9.65% rate for imports.

PIS (Programa de Integração Social): This is the Social Integration Program tax used to fund unemployment insurance and employee bonuses, currently set at 2.10% for imported goods.                            

Please note: In Brazil, the VAT is calculated "inside" its own base. This means the tax is applied to a value that already includes the tax itself.
An 18% nominal rate actually results in an effective tax burden of approximately 21.95% of the total cost because of this "tax-on-tax" math.
Ranges from 17 to 19% but depends on the state (e.g. São Paulo (SP): 18%, Santa Catarina (SC): 17%) In addition, there is an Interstate Rate: If you import the resin into one state (e.g., Santa Catarina) and sell it to a customer in another state (e.g., São Paulo), a fixed interstate rate of 4% usually applies to the sale of imported goods

Import License via the Foreign Trade System (Siscomex). In this system, the importer/broker needs to enter the technical specification of the products (chemical composition, purity level, intended use, etc.).
New plastic products need to contain 22% recycled content by mass since 2026. If you import 100% vPET resin into Brazil, you can comply through recycling credits. 
Chemical regulation (REACH) requirements are gradually required. Safety Data Sheets need to be up to date and import volumes will need to be submitted from 2027.
vPET resin used for food or beverages packaging requires Anvisa (Agência Nacional de Vigilância Sanitária) clearance to confirm that it meets the Mercosur Safety standards.

AEC (The General Rule): This is the "Common External Tariff" agreed upon by Argentina, Brazil, Paraguay, and Uruguay for goods originating from outside the bloc. It represents the standard regional protection level.
DIE (The Exception): Argentina maintains a National List of Exceptions (allowed under MERCOSUR rules until at least 2028). For strategic industrial inputs like PET resin, Argentina often sets the DIE at 6%, even if the general AEC for the region is higher (such as 12.6% or 14%). The DIE is what the importer actually pays to the Argentine customs.
MERCOSUR Origin: Goods originating from member states (Brazil, Uruguay, Paraguay) are generally eligible for a 0% duty rate (Intrazona), provided a valid Certificate of Origin is presented at the time of clearance.
Non-MERCOSUR Origin: For imports from China, India, or the US, the DIE rate (the exception rate) is calculated on the CIF value.
Statistical Fee (Tasa Estadística): In addition to the DIE, a 3% Statistical Fee applies to almost all definitive imports (re-confirmed by Decree 1140/2024 through 2027). 
IIBB is a provincial tax on the sale of goods and services within each jurisdiction. The industrial Rates are Generally 0% to 2%. Because it is a provincial tax, the rate depends on where the sale takes place. If you sell in the City of Buenos Aires, you pay their rate; if you sell in Córdoba, you pay theirs. If your company operates in more than one province, you don't pay the full tax in every province. Instead, you use a formula to distribute your total revenue across the provinces where you have expenses and sales, ensuring you aren't double-taxed on the same dollar. 
Unlike VAT (IVA), IIBB is not a value-added tax. It is "cascading," meaning it is calculated on total revenue at every step of the supply chain without credits for what you paid to suppliers. 

Export duties are applicable on the product but not within the Mercosur countries.

Anti-dumping measures have been removed against China since 30 October 2025.

As of 2025, Argentina repealed the SEDI (statistical import system). This means that for many goods an import license is no longer required and the mere register of import with customs is sufficient. This avoid previous long delays by a more transparent, automatic monitoring system.

No chemical requirements nor any mandatory recycled content requirements.  

vPET resin used food or beverages packaging requires ANMAT (Agência Nacional de Vigilância Sanitária) clearance to confirm that it meets the Mercosur Safety standards.

IVA Anticipado: This is the same as the IVA adicional in Argentina. 
 Income Tax Advance (IRAE): This is a federal Income Tax on Economic Activities payment collected in advance at the point of entry (typically at 4% for standard goods) which the importer uses as a credit against their final annual corporate income tax bill. 
Customer Service Fee: Formally known as the Customs Services Duty, this is an administrative fee of 0.20% of the CIF value (capped at USD 50) charged by the National Customs Directorate to cover the operational costs of processing the import declaration. 
Consular Fee (Tasa Consular): This is a tax applied to the CIF value of imported goods (excluding MERCOSUR origins or specific exemptions) that was originally created to fund consular services but now functions as a general federal revenue tax.Similar to Argentina, but lower tax rates. Uruguay also has the IVA Anticipado which is similar to the IVA adicional.  In addition, Uruguya has the Tasa Consular (Consular Fee) of 5% which is applied to all imports from "Extra-Zone" (Non-MERCOSUR) countries. For MERCOSUR country, this fee is reduced to 3%.

Importers must process their declaration through the VUCE (Ventanilla Única de Comercio Exterior) system, where technical specifications including Intrinsic Viscosity (IV), purity, and chemical grade must be detailed.
PET beverage bottles must contain at least 40% recycled content as of 2026; importers of virgin resin must offset this by local recycling efforts.
Importers must provide a Safety Data Sheet (SDS), and substances are subject to the National Inventory of Chemical Substances being developed under the Comprehensive Waste Management Law.
vPET resin used food or beverages packaging requires MSP (Ministery of Public Health) clearance to confirm that it meets the Mercosur Safety standards.

ISV (Impuesto sobre Ventas): This is the Honduran Value Added Tax (VAT) applied to the sale of goods and services. For imports, it is levied at a standard rate of 15% for Honduras and 13% for El Salvador on the CIF (Cost, Insurance, and Freight) value plus any applicable duties or fees.

Administrative Customs Fee (Tasa de Servicio de Aduanas): This is a service charge for the processing of the import declaration (DUA). It is capped at a maximum of 50 USD per declaration for Honduras and 18 USD for El Salvador and it covers the electronic processing and administrative handling by customs authorities.

Health & Sanitary Permit: For "Food Grade" vPET resin intended for food or beverage contact, importers must obtain a specific health registration/permit.  In Honduras this is regulated by ARSA (Agencia de Regulación Sanitaria). Importers must register the material as a food-contact input. In El Salvado this is  regulated by the Ministry of Health (MINSAL) through its Environmental Safety Division.

Documentation Required: generally, you must provide a Certificate of Analysis (proving atoxicity), a Technical Data Sheet, and evidence that the manufacturing facility follows Good Manufacturing Practices.

ITBIS (Impuesto sobre Transferencias de Bienes Industrializados y Servicios): This is the Dominican Republic’s version of VAT. It is a general consumption tax of 18% applied on the CIF (Cost, Insurance, and Freight) value plus any applicable duties or fees.

Customs Service Fee (Tasa por Servicio Aduanero): This is an ad-valorem fee of 0.4% levied on the CIF value of the goods. It is a dedicated fee used to fund the modernization and operational services of the General Directorate of Customs (DGA).​

The NORDOM 725 standard acts as a mandatory technical regulation for plastic materials in contact with food. It requires that any resin imported for the manufacture of food/beverage packaging must be accompanied by a Food/beverage Grade Certificate or a detailed technical data sheet. This documentation must provide formal proof of the material's safety, physical specifications, and compliance with migration limits to ensure it does not pose a risk to human health.

Note: The full text of NORDOM 725 is not publicly available for free download and must be purchased directly from the INDOCAL (Instituto Dominicano para la Calidad) official portal.

Moreover, Regarding fiscal and operational obligations, Article 36 of Law 225-20 establishes a mandatory annual contribution for all legal entities in the Dominican Republic based on their total annual gross income. This tax, which ranges from 500 RD$ to over 260,000 RD$ depending on the company's revenue, is paid to the DGII to fund the national trust for waste management and infrastructure. Furthermore, Article 122 of the same law mandates that plastic manufacturers must implement recovery and recycling programs for their products and progressively incorporate a specific percentage of recycled resin sourced from the national market into their production processes. This ensures that while you import virgin PET resin, the manufacturing of bottles must legally include a mix of local recycled material to comply with the country's circular economy goals and environmental standards.

Impuesto de Transferencia de Bienes Muebles y Servicios (ITBMS) is the value-added tax (VAT)applied in Panama.  This 7% rate is applied to the sum of [CIF Value + Customs Service Fee + Import Duty].

Customs Service Fee (1%): This is an administrative fee (often referred to as Tasa de Servicio) calculated on the CIF Value. However, this fee is capped at 500 USD. For any shipment with a CIF value over 50,000 USD, the fee remains fixed at 500 USD.

Mandatory "Food Grade" Standards:
Strict Compliance: All materials intended for food or beverage contact must be "Food Grade."
APA Registration: While bulk resin is considered a "raw material" (exempt from full retail sanitary registration), it must still be inscribed in the APA (Panamanian Food Agency) system prior to arrival.

Required Documentation (The Technical Sheet): 
To import resin, you must provide a Technical Data Sheet that is signed, stamped, and on company letterhead. It must include:
Physical, chemical, and microbiological specifications.
Country of origin and manufacturer address.
Shelf life and specific storage conditions.

Import duty=
 Customs value * import duty rate
ICMS(VAT)=ICMS Rate(%) x (Customs Values + Import duty + IPI+PIS+COFINS) / [1 - ICMS Rate(%)]
=18%*('Calculation taxable base'!A4+'Calculation taxable base'!B4+'Calculation taxable base'!C4+'Calculation taxable base'!D4+'Calculation taxable base'!E4)/(1-18%)
=18%*('Calculation taxable base'!A5+B5+'Calculation taxable base'!C5+'Calculation taxable base'!D5+'Calculation taxable base'!E5)/(1-18%)
